1: "Sarai, Abram's wife, had borne him no children. But she had an Egyptian slave (šipḥāh miṣrît) named Hagar" — the three-part statement (barren wife / but / Egyptian slave) sets up the narrative's tension economically. The word šipḥāh (female slave, belonging to the household) identifies Hagar's legal status: she is Sarai's property, not a free person. Her Egyptian nationality connects her to the Egypt episode of Genesis 12 — she may be among the servants acquired from Pharaoh's household (Genesis 12:16). The barrenness of the covenant-mother (noted also for Rebekah, Genesis 25:21, and Rachel, Genesis 29:31) is the recurring crisis through which YHWH demonstrates covenant power operating against biological impossibility.
2: Sarai's proposal — "perhaps I can build a family (ʾibbāneh) through her" — embeds a Hebrew wordplay: the verb bānāh (to build) produces ʾibbāneh (I can be built/get a son), exploiting the phonetic connection between bānāh (build) and bēn (son). The legal background for the proposal is well attested: the Code of Hammurabi (Laws 144-146) and Nuzi adoption tablets (ca. 1500 BCE) describe the practice of a barren wife providing her husband with a slave as surrogate mother, with the resulting child legally recognized as the wife's offspring. Sarai's proposal is not immoral by the standards of her cultural-legal context — it is the recognized ANE legal remedy for barrenness. The moral complexity lies in the conflict between this culturally legitimate solution and the covenant's supernatural fulfillment trajectory.
3: "After Abram had been living in Canaan ten years" — ten years of waiting in the covenant land with no heir despite the repeated promise. The time marker intensifies the crisis: the covenant-promise of 12:2 has been reiterated (Genesis 13:16; Genesis 15:5) but not fulfilled. Sarai "gave" (wattitten) Hagar "to her husband Abram to be his wife (lô lĕʾišāh)" — the formal giving confers on Hagar the legal status of a secondary wife, not merely a surrogate slave. This status elevation will contribute to the social tension of v4: Hagar now has a wife's claims, not merely a slave's subordination. The formal marriage arrangement is the culturally expected legal mechanism, but it creates the social problem the narrative will address.
4: "When she knew she was pregnant, she began to despise (watteqal) her mistress" — the elevation of a slave through pregnancy created well-documented social tensions in the ANE. The Code of Hammurabi (Law 146) addresses this exact situation: a slave given as surrogate who "claims equality with her mistress because she has borne children" may be reduced back to slave status but may not be sold. The verb qālal (to be light/of little weight/of little account) is the opposite of kāḇaḏ (to be heavy, honored) — Hagar is treating Sarai as of little social weight, reversing the honor-hierarchy of mistress and slave. The pregnancy has shifted Hagar's self-perception of her standing in the household.
5: Sarai's complaint — "May the LORD judge (yišpôṭ YHWH) between you and me" — invokes divine arbitration. The formula "may the LORD judge between us" (yišpôṭ YHWH bênî ûḇênekā) is a standard appeal to divine jurisprudence when human resolution is inadequate or unavailable (cf. 1 Samuel 24:12,15: David appeals to YHWH as judge against Saul). Sarai's accusation ("you are responsible for the wrong I am suffering") holds Abram accountable as household head for failing to maintain the social order he was legally responsible to preserve. The Hammurabi Code (Law 146) places the resolution of such conflicts with the husband — Sarai is correct that Abram's passivity is part of the problem.
6: "Your slave is in your hands (yādēk)" — Abram returns Hagar to Sarai's authority. The Hebrew idiom "in your hands" denotes legal jurisdiction and power-over. Abram's abdication of his role as mediator-adjudicator leaves the conflict unresolved and Hagar without protection. "Sarai mistreated (wattĕʿannehā) Hagar" — the Piel of ʿānāh (to oppress/afflict/humble) is the same verb used for Israel's oppression in Egypt (Exodus 1:11,12; Deuteronomy 26:6,7) and for Hagar's own affliction that YHWH "heard" (v11). The narrative echo is pointed: Sarai's treatment of Hagar parallels Egypt's treatment of Israel — the oppressed covenant-household member will become the oppressing household against the household's vulnerable dependent.
7: "The angel of the LORD (malʾak YHWH) found Hagar near a spring in the desert; it was the spring that is beside the road to Shur" — the malʾak YHWH (messenger/angel of YHWH) makes his first appearance in the Bible in this encounter with Hagar. That the first angelic appearance in Scripture is to a runaway Egyptian slave woman — not to a patriarch, a priest, or a warrior — is theologically significant: the divine attention reaches the most marginal figure in the narrative. The road to Shur runs from Canaan through the Sinai toward Egypt — Hagar is heading home. The spring (ʿēn, a perennial spring) in the desert is a life-giving location, marking divine provision in the barren wilderness before any words are spoken.
8: "Hagar, slave of Sarai, where have you come from, and where are you going?" — the angel's double question echoes the pattern of divine interrogation in Genesis: "Where are you?" (3:9 to Adam), "Where is your brother Abel?" (4:9 to Cain). The questions are not requests for information but invitations to honest self-examination and acknowledgment of one's situation. The angel's identification of Hagar by her status ("slave of Sarai") does not diminish her — it acknowledges her real social location. Hagar's transparent answer ("I'm running away from my mistress Sarai") is the honesty that the divine interrogation elicits. She names the reality without evasion.
9: "Go back to your mistress and submit (ʿinnî) to her" — the return command uses the same verb (ʿānāh) that described Sarai's mistreatment of Hagar in v6. YHWH is telling Hagar to return to the source of her oppression and to humble herself before it. The instruction is difficult; its rationale is not given here but becomes clear from the promise that follows (vv10-12): Ishmael's birth and future must occur within the covenant-household structure that Abram and Sarai represent. The return is not an endorsement of Sarai's mistreatment but a providential direction for Hagar's protection and Ishmael's birth in safe conditions.
10: "I will increase (harbāh ʾarbbeh, the double-verb intensifier) your descendants so much that they will be too numerous to count" — the promise to Hagar uses the same uncountable-offspring formula given to Abram (Genesis 13:16; Genesis 15:5). Hagar receives the offspring-promise directly, in the same language as the patriarch — she is not a peripheral figure receiving lesser blessings but a recipient of genuine divine covenant-promise. This is the only instance in Genesis where a woman receives the promise of innumerable descendants directly without male mediation. The Ishmael promise establishes that the Abrahamic covenant's overflow reaches even the excluded-but-related line.
11: "You shall name him Ishmael (Yišmāʿēl), for the LORD has heard (šāmaʿ) your misery (ʿŏnyyēk)" — the name Ishmael = "God hears" (šāmaʿ + ʾēl). The root ʿŏnî (misery/affliction) in the reason-clause uses the same root as Sarai's ʿinnû (mistreated, v6) and Israel's Egyptian ʿānāh (oppression, Exodus 1:11). YHWH heard the cry of the afflicted slave woman and named her son after the divine hearing. The name embeds the memory of divine attention into the child's identity: every time Ishmael's name is spoken, it recalls the moment YHWH heard a marginalized woman's suffering. In this, Ishmael's name-narrative mirrors Israel's — a people defined by the God who heard their cry (Exodus 2:24: "God heard their groaning").
12: "He will be a wild donkey (pere) of a man; his hand will be against everyone and everyone's hand against him, and he will live in hostility toward all his brothers" — the wild donkey (pere) is the biblical symbol for the free, untamable desert nomad (Job 39:5-8: "Who let the wild donkey go free? Who untied its ropes? I gave it the wasteland as its home, the salt flats as its habitat. It laughs at the commotion in the town... It ranges the hills for its pasture"). This is not a curse but a characterization of the Arabian nomadic peoples descended from Ishmael — independent, strong, living by their own terms in the open desert. The phrase "live in hostility toward all his brothers" (ʿal-pĕnê kol-ʾeḥāyw yiškon) can also be translated "he will live in the presence of / alongside all his brothers" — a geographical description of the Arabian peoples dwelling adjacent to but distinct from their settled Semitic neighbors.
13: "You are the God who sees me (ʾAttāh ʾEl rōʾî)" — Hagar's naming of God is unique: she is the only person in the entire OT to give God a name. The theophanic encounter is mutual — YHWH saw Hagar (v13a) and Hagar saw YHWH (v13b: "I have now seen the One who sees me"). The mutual seeing (rāʾāh) is the passage's central theological image: the God of Israel is not only the God of patriarchs but the God who sees every person, even the Egyptian slave woman fleeing through the desert. The name ʾEl Roʾî (the God who sees) will be associated with the well Beer Lahai Roi and later with Isaac's residence (Genesis 24:62; Genesis 25:11) — the covenant patriarch inherits the location where the excluded found divine attention.
14: "That is why the well was called Beer Lahai Roi (bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî)" — "well of the Living One who sees me." The place-name etymology preserves the double divine attribute: YHWH is the Living One (laḥay, a theophanic divine title — cf. ʾĒl Ḥay, the Living God, Hosea 1:10; Psalms 42:2) and the God who sees (rōʾî). The geographic precision — "between Kadesh and Bered" — locates the well in the northern Negev/Sinai boundary region, the same area where Israel will later camp during the wilderness wandering (Kadesh, Numbers 13:26; Genesis 20:1). The well becomes a permanent geographic memorial to the divine-seeing encounter, associated in the text with Isaac's later residence, connecting the covenant-line patriarch to the site of grace given to the excluded.
15: The brief birth narrative — "Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram gave the name Ishmael to the son she had borne" — is notably compact compared to the extended attention given to the theophany in vv7-14. The naming is Abram's: by giving the angel-designated name to the child, Abram acknowledges both the divine revelation to Hagar and his paternity of Ishmael. The formal naming by the father is the ANE act of legal recognition — Abram publicly claims Ishmael as his son, consistent with the secondary-wife legal arrangement of v3. Ishmael's legal status as Abram's heir (under the slave-adoption custom) remains in effect until Isaac's birth (Genesis 21).
16: "Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore him Ishmael" — the age notation maintains the Genesis genealogy's chronological tracking. Abram was 75 at the call (Genesis 12:4), has been in Canaan ten years (Genesis 16:3), and is now 86. The 13 years between Ishmael's birth (86) and the covenant renewal of Genesis 17 (when Abram is 99 and Ishmael is 13) mark a period of silence — the narrative skips from Ishmael's birth directly to the covenant-renewal, suggesting a long waiting period during which Abram presumably regarded Ishmael as the covenant-heir. The 13-year gap before Isaac's promise (Genesis 17:1) is the final extension of the covenant-patience: YHWH will wait 13 more years before revealing that Ishmael is not the covenant-heir.
